dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for a fall.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, I fell down the stairs and got my ankle hurt really badly.
Hi, can you tell me your name?
Yeah, of course-my name is Patricia.
Where are we today, Patricia?
I think we're in Richmond, in my neighborhood near my house.
What's the day and date?
It's Wednesday of August 1, 2020.
Can you tell me what happened today?
I was walking downstairs to get something to drink, missed a step, and fell.
You just opened your eyes when I said your name, which is good.
Patricia, can you describe the pain in your ankle for me?
I can't bear weight on it at all. It's really swollen and red.
Let me check your arms and chest. Are there any marks or rashes you'd like me to see?
I'm looking at your head and neck for injuries. Did you hit your head when you fell?
No, I didn't hit my head.
Look at your ankle-can you wiggle your toes?
Yes, my toes still move fine.
I see a pump attached to your abdomen. Do you use that regularly?
Patricia, how are you breathing today?
It's a little hard to breathe, but I can keep going.
Let me listen to your lungs. Just take a few deep breaths for me.
SpO2 at your finger is 99%.
Respiratory rate is at 14.
Let's see-your skin is pink, warm, and dry with normal turgor. That's usually a good sign.
Blood pressure is 131 over 88.
Heart rate is 109, Patricia. Is that typical for you?
A bit high, but I don't know exactly what mine is normally.
Your airway is patent, and there are no signs of obstruction right now.
Patricia, you're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. That's a GCS of 15.
Your head is normocephalic, and I see no deformities or swelling. There is no jugular vein distension noted.
We're going to take your pulse now, Patricia.
We are going to measure your breathing rate next. Can you just breathe normally?
Patricia, we need your blood pressure now.
Let's check your blood sugar now, Patricia. You might feel a small pinch.
Now, my partner is putting on a clip on your finger to measure oxygen levels. Is that okay?
Now, we'll place a few stickers on your chest to check your heart. Is that okay with you?
Patricia, you mentioned your ankle hurt badly. What else did you feel?
Nausea, and I felt really lightheaded from the fall.
And what happened after you fell?
I tried to get up and get some soda, but I couldn't stand on my ankle.
On a scale of 0 to 10, 10 being the worst pain you ever had, how would you rank the pain in your ankle?
10, absolutely.
Does the pain get worse when you try to move?
Oh yes, and when I try to press on it.
Patricia, can you tell me your medical history?
I have Type 1 diabetes and thyroid issues. I also have hypertension and anemia.
What medications do you take regularly?
Insulin via the pump, but I don't know all the names of my other pills.
Any allergies you'd like us to know?
I'm allergic to penicillin and sulfa drugs. Oh, and I have a latex allergy.
Patricia, we're going to monitor your chest with an ECG strip now. Can you lift your shirt just a little?
I'm going to start an IV in your arm for medication. Can we proceed?
Alright, we gave you some glucose now to get your blood sugar up. How do you feel?
It feels a little better now, less dizzy.
Your blood sugar went from 44 to 53 after the glucose. How do you feel now?
Patricia, we're about to give you some Ondansetron for the nausea.
Your heart rate increased a bit to 116. Is that making you uncomfortable?
Blood pressure is now 136 over 90.
Based on what we know, we'll follow the protocols for diabetic hypoglycemia and general trauma.
I'll administer some Fentanyl next to help with the pain in your ankle.
Your heart rate is still a bit high, but it didn't change much after the medication.
We'll take your vitals again to check how you're responding to treatment. Is that okay?
Pulse is 116, breathing is 18, blood pressure is 136 over 90.
Blood sugar remains 53, still without significant drops.
Let's work on making you more comfortable. I'll get a splint for your ankle now.
We're also putting this ETCO2 cannula to help track your breathing. Is that fine?
This splint will keep your ankle immobilized and prevent more injury. Let me know if it's too tight.
Would you prefer Chippenham Medical Center for your treatment?
We'll help you up on your uninjured leg. Are you ready?
Yes, I trust you.
I'm going to pivot you carefully now. We'll sit you on the stretcher before moving you onto the ambulance.
She's secured and ready to go. Let's load the stretcher into the back of the ambulance.
Patricia, we'll be at Chippenham shortly. They will take good care of you.
I'll transfer her onto the hospital bed now using the sheet pull method.
Surgical history includes diabetes, hypertension, and endometriosis. No prior ED visits for this issue.
Allergies of latex, penicillin, and sulfa reported. No known other food or environmental allergies.
Blood sugar is stable at 53. She was given 15g of glucose, 100mcg Fentanyl, and 4mg of Ondansetron.
No bleeding or transfusion needs. She does have an insulin pump for administration.
No signs of head, neck, or pelvis injury found. Advise ED for ankle dislocation with pain and swelling.
Check systolic and diastolic BP at the ED door-BP 136/90 and HR 116. Monitor closely for trending changes.
Respirations are 18, depth normal. No distress. We're transporting her steadily now.
Oxygen saturation remains 99% despite splinting. No signs of respiratory compromise.
Secondary assessment found no jugular vein distension, and heart rhythm interpreted as sinus tachycardia. Motor strength and sensation are equal and intact in the affected extremity.
Chest symmetric, no distention of JVP noted. Light palpation normal. No auscultation changes.
IV site is good, secure. All nursing care steps preperatory complete.
She's comfortable and showing improvement in pain, nausea, and blood sugar levels.
She didn't take anything for pain within last 24 hours. Last meal 6 hours ago, type was juice.
All care provided includes monitoring, splinting, glucose, Ondansetron, and Fentanyl-nothing omitted.
She seems stable with no acute systemic signs of deterioration in our view. Passing care to your team.
Would you need any additional support from fire or transport crew?
